title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
statement: Design a class to find the K-th largest element in a stream. Note that it is the K-th largest element in the sorted order, not the K-th distinct element.

bruteForce.description: Store all elements in an array. Each time a new element is added, sort the entire array in descending order and return the element at index `K - 1`.

optimized.description: Maintain a Min-Heap of size K containing the K largest elements seen so far. During `add(val)`: push `val` to heap. If heap size exceeds K, pop. The root of the min-heap will always contain the K-th largest element in O(1) time.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Convert input list to min-heap in-place in O(N) time.
The top/root of min-heap contains the smallest value of K largest numbers (K-th largest).

cppExplanation.explanation: Return root of min priority queue.

dryRun: Initial heap size matches K: self.heap = [4, 5, 8]
add(3) -> heap becomes [3, 4, 5, 8] -> pop 3 -> heap is [4, 5, 8]. Return root = 4
add(5) -> heap becomes [4, 5, 5, 8] -> pop 4 -> heap is [5, 5, 8]. Return root = 5
edgeCases: K is larger than initial elements count
followUps: Can you write this using a self-balancing Binary Search Tree? (Yes, search depth O(log N))
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
visualization:
Min-Heap size K keeps K largest. Root is the smallest (K-th largest).
Heap values: [ 5, 5, 8 ] (k=3)
Root element is 5 (K-th largest)
commonMistakes: Using a Max-Heap instead of a Min-Heap, requiring sorting or complete pops to find the K-th element.

title: Last Stone Weight
statement: You are given an array of integers `stones` where `stones[i]` is the weight of the `i`th stone. Smash the two heaviest stones. Return the leftover stone weight. If no stones are left, return 0.

bruteForce.description: Sort the array on each smash step to find the two heaviest elements.

optimized.description: Insert all stones into a Max-Heap. While heap size > 1, pop the two largest stones x and y. If x != y, push the difference `y - x` back into heap. Return 0 if heap is empty, otherwise top value.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Python heapq is min-heap by default. Push negative values to simulate Max-Heap.

cppExplanation.explanation: Initialize max priority queue directly with vector elements.

dryRun: Smash 2 and 1 -> diff = 1. Push 1 -> heap: [1, 1, 1, 1, 1] ... eventually returns 1
followUps: Can you write this iteratively without heap? (Not efficiently, sorting takes O(N^2 log N))
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
visualization:
Extract 2 max -> push difference -> repeat until 1 left
Smash 8, 7 -> push 1. Heap is [4, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1]
commonMistakes: Incorrect sign handling during negation conversions in Python max heaps.

title: Relative Ranks
statement: You are given an integer array `score` of size `n`, where `score[i]` is the score of the `i`th athlete in a competition. All the scores are guaranteed to be unique. Return their relative ranks with custom labels for the top 3.

bruteForce.description: Sort the scores list, then search original positions for indices.

optimized.description: Use a Max-Heap. Push tuples `(score, index)` onto the heap. Pop elements sequentially to assign ranks: first is 'Gold Medal', second 'Silver Medal', third 'Bronze Medal', and the rest as string indices.

pythonCode:
import heapq
def findRelativeRanks(score: list[int]) -> list[str]:
    max_heap = [(-s, i) for i, s in enumerate(score)]
    heapq.heapify(max_heap)
    res = [''] * len(score)
    rank = 1
    while max_heap:
        s, i = heapq.heappop(max_heap)
        if rank == 1: res[i] = 'Gold Medal'
        elif rank == 2: res[i] = 'Silver Medal'
        elif rank == 3: res[i] = 'Bronze Medal'
        else: res[i] = str(rank)
        rank += 1
    return res

pythonExplanation.explanation: Create max-heap pair containing negative score for sorting order and original array index.

cppExplanation.explanation: Push score value and index pair.

spaceComplexity: O(N) - output rank list and heap mapping.
followUps: Can you write this using custom sorting rules? (Yes, sort index arrays based on score values)
visualization:
Max-Heap pops sorted scores -> maps ranks back to original indices
pq = { (10, 0), (9, 3), (8, 2) } -> Pop (10, 0) Gold Medal
commonMistakes: Wrong indices mapping during output assignment.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
statement: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the K-th largest element in the array.

bruteForce.description: Sort the entire array and return index `len - k`.

optimized.description: Maintain a Min-Heap of size K. Iterate through the array: push elements to heap and pop if size exceeds K. The root of heap is K-th largest. Takes O(N log K) time.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Saves overhead by pushing new element and popping root in single atomic execution.

cppExplanation.explanation: Maintain heap capacity at size K.

timeComplexity: O(N log K) - linear search under K limits.
dryRun: Seed heap of size k (2) with first elements: [3, 2] -> min_heap = [2, 3]
followUps: Can you write this using QuickSelect? (Yes, average O(N) time and O(1) space, but worst case is O(N^2))
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
visualization:
Min-Heap size K holds K largest. Root is the smallest (Kth largest).
heap = [ 5, 6 ] -> Root is 5
commonMistakes: Using a Max-Heap of size N which consumes O(N) space and takes O(N log N) worst-case time to pop K times.
whenNotToUse: If memory is critical, QuickSelect has O(1) auxiliary space.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
statement: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the `k` most frequent elements.

bruteForce.description: Count frequencies, sort frequencies list in descending order, and return top K keys.

optimized.description: Count frequencies in map. Push pairs `(frequency, element)` onto a Min-Heap. Keep heap size at K by popping smaller frequencies. Return remaining heap elements.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Map frequency as first element in heap tuple to ensure comparison by occurrence count.

cppExplanation.explanation: Pair of {frequency, value} pushed to heap.

timeComplexity: O(N log K) - linear processing under K limits.
edgeCases: Frequencies of all elements are identical
followUps: Can you write this in O(N) using Bucket Sort? (Yes, map frequencies to lists of values)
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
visualization:
Map frequencies -> Min-Heap size K sorting -> collect values
heap = [ (2, 2), (3, 1) ] -> Remaining values are 2 and 1
commonMistakes: Using values instead of frequencies as sorting criteria inside Heap.
whenNotToUse: If unique count is close to N, Bucket Sort is more efficient.

title: Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums
statement: You are given two integer arrays `nums1` and `nums2` sorted in non-decreasing order and an integer `k`. Find `k` pairs with the smallest sums.

bruteForce.description: Generate all pairs, sort them, and return top K.

optimized.description: Use a Min-Heap. Push pairs `(sum, index1, index2)`. Initially insert `(nums1[i] + nums2[0], i, 0)` for all `i`. Pop min sum, add pair to output, and push next candidate index `(nums1[i] + nums2[j+1], i, j+1)`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Optimization: only seed first K elements as further indices exceed K result bounds.
Push sum and updated index pair to trace next smallest candidate sum.

cppExplanation.explanation: Insert next sorted combination pair onto heap.

timeComplexity: O(K log K) - performs K extraction steps.
edgeCases: K is larger than total pairs count
followUps: Can you write this using binary search? (Yes, search for Kth smallest sum value)
relatedProblems: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
visualization:
Seed heap with starting pairs -> pop min -> push next index adjacent pair
heap = [ (3,0,0), (9,1,0) ] -> Pop (3,0,0), push (5,0,1)
commonMistakes: Pushing all index pairs initially which leads to Out of Memory on large arrays.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
statement: Design a data structure that supports adding numbers from a data stream and finding the median of all numbers added so far in O(1) time.

bruteForce.description: Sort the stream array on each median lookup query.

optimized.description: Maintain two heaps: a Max-Heap for the lower half numbers, and a Min-Heap for the upper half. Ensure heights differ by at most 1. If max_heap top > min_heap top, swap them. Median is either top of larger heap or average of both tops.
optimized.timeComplexity: O(log N) insert, O(1) median query

cppExplanation.explanation: Enforce values in small heap are always <= values in large heap.

dryRun: 2. add(2): small=[-2], large=[] -> small size > large size + 1 -> Pop 2 from small, push to large. small=[-1], large=[2]
4. add(3): small=[-3, -1], large=[2] -> 3 > 2 -> swap -> small=[-2, -1], large=[3]. Balance size: pop 2 from small, push to large. small=[-1], large=[2, 3]. Returns 2.0
followUps: Can you write this using dynamic tree array bucket splits? (No, heaps are optimal)
visualization:
Max-Heap (small half) | Min-Heap (large half) -> balance sizes -> median from roots
small: [ 1, 2 ] | large: [ 3, 4 ] -> roots are 2 and 3
commonMistakes: Incorrect size imbalance check or swapping signs during negations in Python max heaps.

title: Merge k Sorted Lists
statement: You are given an array of `k` linked-lists `lists`, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list.

bruteForce.description: Compare list heads sequentially in loops. (O(N * K) time).

optimized.description: Use a Min-Heap. Insert head node of all K lists. Pop node, append it to result, and insert its next node. Repeat until heap is empty.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Push tuple containing value, index i (avoids duplicate comparisons error on list nodes) and node reference.

cppExplanation.explanation: Configure Min-Priority queue using custom comparator operator struct.

dryRun: Step 1: pop (1, 0). curr = node1a. Push next (4, 0, node1b) -> heap = [(1, 1, node2a), (4, 0, node1b)]
Step 2: pop (1, 1). curr = node1a -> node2a. Push next (3, 1, node2b) -> heap = [(3, 1, node2b), (4, 0, node1b)]
followUps: Can you write this using Divide and Conquer? (Yes, merge lists in pairs)
visualization: Heap keeps current heads sorted -> Pop min -> Push next -> repeat
commonMistakes: Custom comparisons compile bugs in C++ or duplicate node reference comparison in Python.

title: Skyline Problem
statement: A city's skyline is the outer contour of the silhouette formed by all the buildings in that city when viewed from a distance. Given the locations of buildings, return the key contours skyline coordinates.

bruteForce.description: Generate all X coordinates and scan all overlapping buildings manually. (O(N^2) time).

optimized.description: Sort coordinates events. Left edge: `(start, -height, end)`. Right edge: `(end, 0, None)`. Maintain active building heights in a Max-Heap. When left edge is met, push height. When right edge is met, remove building. Compare max height changes to record skyline corners.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Sort events by X coordinate. Ties resolved naturally by height sorting order.
Pop building heights that are no longer active at current X position.

cppExplanation.explanation: Insert active building height onto sorting multiset (acts as Max-Heap).

timeComplexity: O(N log N) - sorting and heap operations.
followUps: Can you solve it using a Segment Tree? (Yes, but memory is O(N))
visualization:
Event sorting -> add/remove heights -> track max height changes to draw contour corners
active heap = { (-15, 7), (-10, 9) } -> root is -15
commonMistakes: Incorrect event sorting, resulting in drawing vertical overlap mistakes.
